text: Redis 6.2 cluster setup uses the requirepass directive together with masterauth so that every node in the cluster shares a single password-based authentication flow. Older guides recommend that operators use requirepass in redis.conf on each node before creating the cluster.

Redis 7 cluster documentation began steering users toward ACL-aware authentication. Legacy requirepass examples still existed for compatibility, but the recommended direction was to create named users and align cluster node credentials with ACL rules instead of relying on a single global password.

Redis 8 cluster deployment guidance deprecated requirepass-based cluster authentication and removed it from the recommended setup path. Current cluster configuration expects ACL-managed users, explicit user permissions, and node-to-node authentication that follows the ACL configuration instead of a shared requirepass value.

Redis 6 authentication guidance centers on a single requirepass secret for clients plus masterauth for replication links. Operational playbooks usually describe one shared password rather than named users with separate permissions.

Redis 7 keeps compatibility with requirepass but pushes operators toward ACL users, category-based permissions, and separate credentials per role. Security guidance increasingly treats the single-password pattern as transitional rather than ideal.

Redis 8 operational guidance does not rely on one shared requirepass secret for authentication. Recommended deployments define ACL users, scope permissions by user, and manage node-to-node access with ACL identities instead of a single global password.

Redis 6 persistence documentation recommends combining RDB snapshots with appendonly yes when you need both quick restarts and stronger durability guarantees. The guidance focuses on balancing snapshot intervals with append-only rewrite cost.

Redis 8 persistence guidance still supports RDB snapshots and the append-only file, but newer operational recommendations emphasize validating rewrite pressure, disk throughput, and recovery objectives during deployment. The persistence model itself remains available and supported.

Redis 6 stores working data in memory and depends on maxmemory plus eviction policy choices to control pressure. Capacity planning guides focus on RAM sizing, dataset overhead, and replication buffers.

Redis 8 keeps hot data in RAM and continues to rely on memory-efficient data structures for low-latency access. Capacity planning guidance focuses on maxmemory sizing, eviction policy choice, and operating headroom for replication and persistence overhead.

Redis 6 replication setup uses replicaof together with masterauth when the primary requires a password. Operational runbooks describe configuring every replica with the shared primary password before synchronization.

Redis 8 replication guidance expects ACL-aware credentials for replica connections and recommends role-specific users instead of one shared password. The configuration still uses replicaof, but authentication policy follows ACL identity management.

Redis 6 TLS deployment documentation covers enabling tls-port, distributing certificates, and keeping the plaintext port disabled in hardened environments. The guidance treats TLS as optional but recommended for production traffic.

Redis 8 TLS deployment still uses tls-port and certificate-based setup, while newer operations guidance emphasizes rotating certificates, validating client identity, and monitoring encrypted connection overhead. TLS remains supported and recommended.
